message.content: aquaint

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	# Todo
2	
3	This file contains near term tasks with a short description
4	and reference links to more details.
5	
6	## In Progress
7	
8	A bulleted list of the in-progress task's development "ladder":
9	   - 0.xx.y-0 blah (done)
10	   - 0.xx.y-1 blah blah (current)
11	   - 0.xx.y-2 blah blah blah
12	   - 0.xx.y close-out and validation
13	
14	- 0.42.0-0 plan + version bump + new chores-07.md (done) [76]
15	- 0.42.0-1 scope.rs enum — `Roles(Vec<Side>) | Single(PathBuf)` (done) [76]
16	- 0.42.0-2 custom CLI parser + retrofit `init` --scope (done) [76]
17	- 0.42.0-3 retrofit `sync` — drop -R, add -s (done) [76]
18	- 0.42.0-4 push --scope (state-machine becomes scope-aware) [76]
19	  - 0.42.0-4.5 substep protocol + jj revsets (done) [77]
20	  - 0.42.0-4.6 init-clone-refactor recovery (done) [78]
21	  - 0.42.0-4.7 init-clone-refactor rebase landing (current) [79]
22	- 0.42.0-5 finalize --scope (replaces --repo) [76]
23	- 0.42.0-6 clone --scope [76]
24	- 0.42.0-7 Single(_) dogfood validation [76]
25	- 0.42.0 close-out [76]
26	
27	## Todo
28	
29	A markdown list of tasks to do in the near future, ordered
30	highest-priority first. Keep entries brief — 1-3 lines.
31	Detailed motivation, safety requirements, and ordering belong
32	in `notes/chores-NN.md` design subsections; link via `[N]` ref.
33	
34	Items use lazy numbering — every entry begins with `1. `; the
35	markdown renderer auto-numbers them, so reorder/insert without
36	renumbering. Reference by displayed number ("let's work on #3").
37	1. Test-module extraction across oversized files. Convert
38	   `src/init.rs` → `src/init/mod.rs` + `src/init/tests.rs`
39	   (sibling-submodule pattern; tests still reach private
40	   items via `use super::*;`). Same shape for `push.rs`
41	   (785 test lines), `sync.rs` (673), `common.rs` (361,
42	   borderline). Pure mechanical reshape, no behavior
43	   change. One sub-step per file. Candidate cycle: 0.41.2
44	   or its own. Splitting tests first makes a follow-on
45	   DRY walk across init/push/sync easier to read.
46	1. **Rebase note — CLAUDE.md `### Per-file review checkpoints`.**
47	   Both `main` (0.42.0 work) and `init-clone-refactor`
48	   (0.41.1) authored this subsection independently —
49	   same intent, different wording. When 0.42.0 rebases on
50	   top of 0.41.1 at close-out, resolve CAREFULLY: don't
51	   take either side wholesale, reconcile to preserve the
52	   best of both. Likely conflict surface is the bullet
53	   list under "How to apply".
54	1. **Symmetric `.vc-config.toml` schema.** Add `code = "/"`
55	   and `bot = "/.claude"` (workspace-root-relative paths) so
56	   both repos read from the same shape. Side detection walks
57	   up from cwd via existing `find_workspace_root`, then maps
58	   cwd-relative-to-root onto the configured `code` / `bot`
59	   paths. Cwd-basename match is a fast-path shortcut at
60	   role-root level only — subdirs need the walk-up. Was the
61	   deferred half of `0.41.1-6`'s title. Migration story for
62	   existing workspaces TBD at design time. [75]
63	1. **`test_helpers::Fixture` migration + downstream callers.**
64	   Plus rename `Fixture` → `TestFixtureDual` and `FixturePor`
65	   → `TestFixturePor` so call sites carry the test-only
66	   signal that `#[cfg(test)] mod test_helpers` doesn't
67	   communicate. Was `0.41.1-7`. [73]
68	1. **init dual|por arg split.** Via `#[command(flatten)]` of
69	   `ProvisionOptionFlagBundle` (built in -6.7) +
70	   `provision_side(role, …)` shared helper. CLI surface
71	   decision (subcommands `init dual|por` vs preserved
72	   `--scope` flag with manual two-pass parse) deferred to
73	   design time. Was `0.41.1-6.9`; may or may not happen.
74	1. Decide merge direction for `init-clone-refactor`
75	   (recovered 2026-05-02). Rebase `0.41.1` onto
76	   `0.42.0` → `0.43.0`-or-`0.42.1`, vs force-rewrite
77	   main, vs other topology. [78]
78	1. Cross-file chores-NN ordering sanity pass at
79	   `init-clone-refactor` merge time. Bring chores-08.md
80	   (0.41.1 per-step subsections) onto main alongside
81	   chores-06/07; normalize section ordering across all
82	   three. Tied to merge-direction decision above. [78]
83	1. Add a vc-x1 validate-repo?
84	1. vc-x1 push: `--scope=code|bot|code,bot|<path>` flag.
85	   Lands in the 0.42.0 cycle alongside the enum
86	   refactor; state machine becomes scope-aware (single-
87	   side path skips `commit-claude`/bookmark-claude/
88	   `finalize-claude`; `Single(_)` is single-repo mode).
89	   [57],[60],[71],[72]
90	1. vc-x1 clone: `--scope=code|bot|code,bot|<path>` flag.
91	   Parallel to `init --scope`; single-repo clone target
92	   via the path form. 0.42.0 cycle. [60],[71],[72]
93	1. vc-x1 validate-desc / fix-desc:
94	   `--scope=code|bot|code,bot` flag. Same role vocabulary
95	   as elsewhere — `code` validates code's commits against
96	   bot, `bot` reverses, `code,bot` does both (new
97	   default). `Single(_)` errors here (validate compares
98	   two repos by definition). 0.42.0 cycle. [60],[71],[72]
99	1. CommonArgs sweep — add `--scope=code|bot|code,bot|<path>`
100	   to `chid`/`desc`/`list`/`show` in one cycle (single
101	   shared `CommonArgs` change picks all four up). Drops
102	   the existing `-R`/`--repo` repeatable flag in favor of
103	   the new path form. 0.42.0 cycle. [60],[71],[76]
104	1. Unify `.vc-config.toml` accessors onto Pattern B
105	   (typed struct + `load_from(path)`, like new
106	   `config::UserConfig` and `push::resolve_state_layout`).
107	   Replaces the map-typed helpers in `desc_helpers.rs` /
108	   `fix_desc.rs` / `validate_desc.rs` with a typed
109	   `WorkspaceConfig` struct. ~50 LOC, mechanical.
110	   Candidate for 0.41.2. [74]
111	1. Layered config precedence (user → workspace → CLI)
112	   once `WorkspaceConfig` is typed. Workspace can
113	   override `[github].owner` etc. for a specific project;
114	   init can't use the layer (chicken-and-egg) but
115	   post-init commands can. Depends on the
116	   `WorkspaceConfig` typed-struct refactor above.
117	   Candidate for 0.41.2. [74]
118	1. Help layout: force over-under everywhere. Apply
119	   `next_line_help(true)` at the root (or via the existing
120	   `cli_with_banner` walker) so every subcommand's `-h` /
121	   `--help` uses the same layout. Today clap auto-picks
122	   per-command based on the widest flag spec, so
123	   `sync -h` is two-column but `init -h` is over-under —
124	   visual inconsistency.
125	1. Replace "Step N" log prefixes with single-word
126	   `label: body` convention (`bookmark`, `provision`,
127	   `colocate`, `cross-ref`, `symlink`, …); indent labels
128	   under per-side `code:` / `bot:` headers in dual.
129	   Originally planned as 0.41.1-6.7; deferred.
130	1. Consider renaming the `.vc-config.toml` `[workspace]`
131	   section. Rust readers expect `[workspace]` to mean a
132	   Cargo workspace, which a vc-x1 dual-repo isn't.
133	   Candidates: `[repo-list]`, `[project]`, `[dual-repo]`.
134	   Breaking change — needs migration story (read both
135	   names during a transition cycle, or one-shot rewrite
136	   in `vc-x1 sync`/`init` on first contact). Drives the
137	   broader "stop saying workspace in user-facing surfaces"
138	   sweep.
139	1. Add `status` (alias `st`) subcommand: `jj st` across both
140	   repos in one shot. Uses `--scope` from day one. This is
141	   natural home for the working-copy signal called out and
142	   it needs to include remotes, like remotes/origin/main. [54].
143	1. `vc-x1 init --dry-run` should bypass the
144	   `--repo-remote` path-existence preflight (currently fires
145	   before the dry-run early-return; observed dogfooding
146	   2026-04-24).
147	1. vc-x1 push: `--squash` flag. Squashes WC into `@-` via
148	   `--ignore-immutable` and force-pushes; needs
149	   `--force-with-lease`-equivalent + state-sanity preflight in
150	   place first. [57]
151	1. vc-x1 push: `--message-file PATH` flag. Git-style commit
152	   message file (first line = title, blank, rest = body).
153	   Alternative to `--title` + `--body`. [58]
154	1. Mirror `--check` / `--no-check` onto `vc-x1 push` (forwards
155	   through to the preflight `vc-x1 sync` invocation).
156	   0.37.1 hard-codes `--check`; default stays `--check`.
157	1. Add `validate-repo` subcommand: diagnostic that runs all
158	   `verify_*` checks (tracking, push state freshness, ochid
159	   integrity, conflicts, config sanity, working-copy state)
160	   and reports per-check pass/fail. Exit code = number of
161	   failed checks. Implementation: promote
162	   `verify_state_sanity` / `verify_completion_sanity` from
163	   push.rs to `common.rs`. [69]
164	1. sync: surface working-copy state in the up-to-date summary
165	   (per-repo pending-files count or compact stat). Wording-only
166	   fix shipped in 0.37.1; this is the design+impl. [54]
167	1. bm-track silent-when-clean refinement. Print on entry/exit
168	   only when state isn't fully tracked or when exit state
169	   differs from entry. [62]
170	1. "Oh shit" revert — post-success undo via `.vc-x1-ops/`
171	   anchor dir. Idea-stage; every repo-mutating command drops a
172	   pre-op snapshot, `vc-x1 undo` restores both repos. [57]
173	1. Restructure templates: replace separate `vc-template-x1` +
174	   `vc-template-x1.claude` repos with a single `vc-template-x1`
175	   that has `.claude/` as a subdir (covers `LICENSE-*` etc. for
176	   both sides in one place). Updates to `vc-x1 init` / `clone`
177	   needed for the new layout.
178	1. Source-code design ref sweep + CLAUDE.md codification:
179	   adopt section-name + `blob/main/...` URL pattern for source
180	   code refs to designs; codify in CLAUDE.md alongside the
181	   existing markdown ref conventions. Sweep targets:
182	   src/push.rs lines 4, 121, 645, 1219. [68]
183	1. Richer bookmark enumeration: per-bookmark remote presence + tracking status [52]
184	1. Per-line/per-thread runtime log points (future, maybe) [36]
185	1. Add Windows symlink support via `std::os::windows::fs::symlink_dir` [37]
186	1. Add "::" revision syntax for jj compatibility
187	1. Add -p, --parents, -c, --children so parent and child counts can be asymmetric
188	1. Add integration tests in tests/ for subcommands using temp jj repos (tempfile crate)
189	1. Fix .claude repo history: dev0 through dev2 sessions squashed into wrong commit [4],[5]
190	1. Add `vc-x1 setup` subcommand: completions install, .claude repo init, symlink setup [27]
191	1. Add dynamic revision completion via `ArgValueCompleter` (jj doesn't complete revsets either) [28],[29]
192	1. Test-tempdir override resolution chain. Both
193	   `src/test_helpers::unique_base` and
194	   `tests/common/unique_base` currently use
195	   `std::env::temp_dir()` (= `$TMPDIR`). Generalize to
196	   resolve in priority order: explicit env var (e.g.
197	   `VC_X1_TEST_TMPDIR`) → user config
198	   (`~/.config/vc-x1/config.toml`) → local
199	   `.vc-config.toml` → `std::env::temp_dir()` fallback.
200	   Useful when a developer wants tests on a tmpfs / SSD /
201	   project-local path without exporting `TMPDIR` globally.
202	   Open question: do we also expose a CLI parameter
203	   (e.g. `vc-x1 --workspace-tmp …`)? Test binaries can't
204	   easily accept arbitrary flags via `cargo test --`, so
205	   env is the realistic surface for tests; for the
206	   `vc-x1` binary itself a flag is feasible but unclear
207	   it adds value over the resolution chain.
208	
209	## Done
210

212	and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

# Todo

This file contains near term tasks with a short description
and reference links to more details.

## In Progress

A bulleted list of the in-progress task's development "ladder":
   - 0.xx.y-0 blah (done)
   - 0.xx.y-1 blah blah (current)
   - 0.xx.y-2 blah blah blah
   - 0.xx.y close-out and validation

- 0.42.0-0 plan + version bump + new chores-07.md (done) [76]
- 0.42.0-1 scope.rs enum — `Roles(Vec<Side>) | Single(PathBuf)` (done) [76]
- 0.42.0-2 custom CLI parser + retrofit `init` --scope (done) [76]
- 0.42.0-3 retrofit `sync` — drop -R, add -s (done) [76]
- 0.42.0-4 push --scope (state-machine becomes scope-aware) [76]
  - 0.42.0-4.5 substep protocol + jj revsets (done) [77]
  - 0.42.0-4.6 init-clone-refactor recovery (done) [78]
  - 0.42.0-4.7 init-clone-refactor rebase landing (current) [79]
- 0.42.0-5 finalize --scope (replaces --repo) [76]
- 0.42.0-6 clone --scope [76]
- 0.42.0-7 Single(_) dogfood validation [76]
- 0.42.0 close-out [76]

## Todo

A markdown list of tasks to do in the near future, ordered
highest-priority first. Keep entries brief — 1-3 lines.
Detailed motivation, safety requirements, and ordering belong
in `notes/chores-NN.md` design subsections; link via `[N]` ref.

Items use lazy numbering — every entry begins with `1. `; the
markdown renderer auto-numbers them, so reorder/insert without
renumbering. Reference by displayed number ("let's work on #3").
1. Test-module extraction across oversized files. Convert
   `src/init.rs` → `src/init/mod.rs` + `src/init/tests.rs`
   (sibling-submodule pattern; tests still reach private
   items via `use super::*;`). Same shape for `push.rs`
   (785 test lines), `sync.rs` (673), `common.rs` (361,
   borderline). Pure mechanical reshape, no behavior
   change. One sub-step per file. Candidate cycle: 0.41.2
   or its own. Splitting tests first makes a follow-on
   DRY walk across init/push/sync easier to read.
1. **Rebase note — CLAUDE.md `### Per-file review checkpoints`.**
   Both `main` (0.42.0 work) and `init-clone-refactor`
   (0.41.1) authored this subsection independently —
   same intent, different wording. When 0.42.0 rebases on
   top of 0.41.1 at close-out, resolve CAREFULLY: don't
   take either side wholesale, reconcile to preserve the
   best of both. Likely conflict surface is the bullet
   list under "How to apply".
1. **Symmetric `.vc-config.toml` schema.** Add `code = "/"`
   and `bot = "/.claude"` (workspace-root-relative paths) so
   both repos read from the same shape. Side detection walks
   up from cwd via existing `find_workspace_root`, then maps
   cwd-relative-to-root onto the configured `code` / `bot`
   paths. Cwd-basename match is a fast-path shortcut at
   role-root level only — subdirs need the walk-up. Was the
   deferred half of `0.41.1-6`'s title. Migration story for
   existing workspaces TBD at design time. [75]
1. **`test_helpers::Fixture` migration + downstream callers.**
   Plus rename `Fixture` → `TestFixtureDual` and `FixturePor`
   → `TestFixturePor` so call sites carry the test-only
   signal that `#[cfg(test)] mod test_helpers` doesn't
   communicate. Was `0.41.1-7`. [73]
1. **init dual|por arg split.** Via `#[command(flatten)]` of
   `ProvisionOptionFlagBundle` (built in -6.7) +
   `provision_side(role, …)` shared helper. CLI surface
   decision (subcommands `init dual|por` vs preserved
   `--scope` flag with manual two-pass parse) deferred to
   design time. Was `0.41.1-6.9`; may or may not happen.
1. Decide merge direction for `init-clone-refactor`
   (recovered 2026-05-02). Rebase `0.41.1` onto
   `0.42.0` → `0.43.0`-or-`0.42.1`, vs force-rewrite
   main, vs other topology. [78]
1. Cross-file chores-NN ordering sanity pass at
   `init-clone-refactor` merge time. Bring chores-08.md
   (0.41.1 per-step subsections) onto main alongside
   chores-06/07; normalize section ordering across all
   three. Tied to merge-direction decision above. [78]
1. Add a vc-x1 validate-repo?
1. vc-x1 push: `--scope=code|bot|code,bot|<path>` flag.
   Lands in the 0.42.0 cycle alongside the enum
   refactor; state machine becomes scope-aware (single-
   side path skips `commit-claude`/bookmark-claude/
   `finalize-claude`; `Single(_)` is single-repo mode).
   [57],[60],[71],[72]
1. vc-x1 clone: `--scope=code|bot|code,bot|<path>` flag.
   Parallel to `init --scope`; single-repo clone target
   via the path form. 0.42.0 cycle. [60],[71],[72]
1. vc-x1 validate-desc / fix-desc:
   `--scope=code|bot|code,bot` flag. Same role vocabulary
   as elsewhere — `code` validates code's commits against
   bot, `bot` reverses, `code,bot` does both (new
   default). `Single(_)` errors here (validate compares
   two repos by definition). 0.42.0 cycle. [60],[71],[72]
1. CommonArgs sweep — add `--scope=code|bot|code,bot|<path>`
   to `chid`/`desc`/`list`/`show` in one cycle (single
   shared `CommonArgs` change picks all four up). Drops
   the existing `-R`/`--repo` repeatable flag in favor of
   the new path form. 0.42.0 cycle. [60],[71],[76]
1. Unify `.vc-config.toml` accessors onto Pattern B
   (typed struct + `load_from(path)`, like new
   `config::UserConfig` and `push::resolve_state_layout`).
   Replaces the map-typed helpers in `desc_helpers.rs` /
   `fix_desc.rs` / `validate_desc.rs` with a typed
   `WorkspaceConfig` struct. ~50 LOC, mechanical.
   Candidate for 0.41.2. [74]
1. Layered config precedence (user → workspace → CLI)
   once `WorkspaceConfig` is typed. Workspace can
   override `[github].owner` etc. for a specific project;
   init can't use the layer (chicken-and-egg) but
   post-init commands can. Depends on the
   `WorkspaceConfig` typed-struct refactor above.
   Candidate for 0.41.2. [74]
1. Help layout: force over-under everywhere. Apply
   `next_line_help(true)` at the root (or via the existing
   `cli_with_banner` walker) so every subcommand's `-h` /
   `--help` uses the same layout. Today clap auto-picks
   per-command based on the widest flag spec, so
   `sync -h` is two-column but `init -h` is over-under —
   visual inconsistency.
1. Replace "Step N" log prefixes with single-word
   `label: body` convention (`bookmark`, `provision`,
   `colocate`, `cross-ref`, `symlink`, …); indent labels
   under per-side `code:` / `bot:` headers in dual.
   Originally planned as 0.41.1-6.7; deferred.
1. Consider renaming the `.vc-config.toml` `[workspace]`
   section. Rust readers expect `[workspace]` to mean a
   Cargo workspace, which a vc-x1 dual-repo isn't.
   Candidates: `[repo-list]`, `[project]`, `[dual-repo]`.
   Breaking change — needs migration story (read both
   names during a transition cycle, or one-shot rewrite
   in `vc-x1 sync`/`init` on first contact). Drives the
   broader "stop saying workspace in user-facing surfaces"
   sweep.
1. Add `status` (alias `st`) subcommand: `jj st` across both
   repos in one shot. Uses `--scope` from day one. This is
   natural home for the working-copy signal called out and
   it needs to include remotes, like remotes/origin/main. [54].
1. `vc-x1 init --dry-run` should bypass the
   `--repo-remote` path-existence preflight (currently fires
   before the dry-run early-return; observed dogfooding
   2026-04-24).
1. vc-x1 push: `--squash` flag. Squashes WC into `@-` via
   `--ignore-immutable` and force-pushes; needs
   `--force-with-lease`-equivalent + state-sanity preflight in
   place first. [57]
1. vc-x1 push: `--message-file PATH` flag. Git-style commit
   message file (first line = title, blank, rest = body).
   Alternative to `--title` + `--body`. [58]
1. Mirror `--check` / `--no-check` onto `vc-x1 push` (forwards
   through to the preflight `vc-x1 sync` invocation).
   0.37.1 hard-codes `--check`; default stays `--check`.
1. Add `validate-repo` subcommand: diagnostic that runs all
   `verify_*` checks (tracking, push state freshness, ochid
   integrity, conflicts, config sanity, working-copy state)
   and reports per-check pass/fail. Exit code = number of
   failed checks. Implementation: promote
   `verify_state_sanity` / `verify_completion_sanity` from
   push.rs to `common.rs`. [69]
1. sync: surface working-copy state in the up-to-date summary
   (per-repo pending-files count or compact stat). Wording-only
   fix shipped in 0.37.1; this is the design+impl. [54]
1. bm-track silent-when-clean refinement. Print on entry/exit
   only when state isn't fully tracked or when exit state
   differs from entry. [62]
1. "Oh shit" revert — post-success undo via `.vc-x1-ops/`
   anchor dir. Idea-stage; every repo-mutating command drops a
   pre-op snapshot, `vc-x1 undo` restores both repos. [57]
1. Restructure templates: replace separate `vc-template-x1` +
   `vc-template-x1.claude` repos with a single `vc-template-x1`
   that has `.claude/` as a subdir (covers `LICENSE-*` etc. for
   both sides in one place). Updates to `vc-x1 init` / `clone`
   needed for the new layout.
1. Source-code design ref sweep + CLAUDE.md codification:
   adopt section-name + `blob/main/...` URL pattern for source
   code refs to designs; codify in CLAUDE.md alongside the
   existing markdown ref conventions. Sweep targets:
   src/push.rs lines 4, 121, 645, 1219. [68]
1. Richer bookmark enumeration: per-bookmark remote presence + tracking status [52]
1. Per-line/per-thread runtime log points (future, maybe) [36]
1. Add Windows symlink support via `std::os::windows::fs::symlink_dir` [37]
1. Add "::" revision syntax for jj compatibility
1. Add -p, --parents, -c, --children so parent and child counts can be asymmetric
1. Add integration tests in tests/ for subcommands using temp jj repos (tempfile crate)
1. Fix .claude repo history: dev0 through dev2 sessions squashed into wrong commit [4],[5]
1. Add `vc-x1 setup` subcommand: completions install, .claude repo init, symlink setup [27]
1. Add dynamic revision completion via `ArgValueCompleter` (jj doesn't complete revsets either) [28],[29]
1. Test-tempdir override resolution chain. Both
   `src/test_helpers::unique_base` and
   `tests/common/unique_base` currently use
   `std::env::temp_dir()` (= `$TMPDIR`). Generalize to
   resolve in priority order: explicit env var (e.g.
   `VC_X1_TEST_TMPDIR`) → user config
   (`~/.config/vc-x1/config.toml`) → local
   `.vc-config.toml` → `std::env::temp_dir()` fallback.
   Useful when a developer wants tests on a tmpfs / SSD /
   project-local path without exporting `TMPDIR` globally.
   Open question: do we also expose a CLI parameter
   (e.g. `vc-x1 --workspace-tmp …`)? Test binaries can't
   easily accept arbitrary flags via `cargo test --`, so
   env is the realistic surface for tests; for the
   `vc-x1` binary itself a flag is feasible but unclear
   it adds value over the resolution chain.

## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

- Substep protocol formalization + jj revsets cheatsheet (0.42.0-4.5) [77]
- init-clone-refactor recovery + post-mortem playbook (0.42.0-4.6) [78]

content.text:
Acquainted. Quick read on where things stand:

**Project:** `vc-x1` — Rust CLI for managing dual jj-git repos (app + `.claude`) with subcommands like `init`, `clone`, `sync`, `push`, `finalize`, `chid`, `desc`, `validate-desc`, etc. v0.42.0-4.7 just landed.

**Repo layout:** App at `/`, bot session at `/.claude`. Both jj-managed. Working copy at `@` is empty on top of `main` (`bdec8579` — `chore: init-clone-refactor rebase landing (0.42.0-4.7)`).

**In Progress:** Multi-step `0.42.0` cycle — `--scope` enum refactor across subcommands. Steps 0–4 done (incl. recent 4.5 substep protocol, 4.6 recovery, 4.7 rebase landing). Next up: `0.42.0-5 finalize --scope` (replaces `--repo`).

**Workflow conventions** (from CLAUDE.md):
- Commit-first review on sub-steps; per-file checkpoints for top-level work.
- `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` for the commit→push→finalize flow with two approval gates.
- "Stop and wait" after finalize — no auto-proceed.
- "The bot thinks ..." marker for speculation in durable text.
- Doc comments on every file/fn/method; `// OK: ...` justification on `unwrap*` calls.

Ready when you are. What's next — start `0.42.0-5 finalize --scope`, or something else?

content: Goal is the 0.42.0 --scope enum refactor across vc-x1 subcommands; steps 0 through 4 are done (last landing was 4.7). Next action is starting 0.42.0-5, retrofitting finalize with --scope to replace --repo. (disable recaps in /config)

message.content:
So we've added the notion of "shared' cli args, but that has caused an issue with the coding, where we have
`args.xxx.xxx` such as `args.account.account` etc that you can see below:
```
pub(crate) fn plan_init(
    args: &InitArgs,
    cfg: &UserConfig,
) -> Result<InitPlan, Box<dyn std::error::Error>> {
    debug!(
        "init args: target={:?}, name={:?}, account={:?}, repo={:?}, scope={:?}, private={}",
        args.target,
        args.name,
        args.account.account,
        args.repo.repo,
        args.scope.scope,
        args.provision.private.private
    );
  ..
```

I've got an idea, maybe use a "context" parameter instead of passing args directly.
But maybe you can come up with some other solution. In addition, I'm wondering
if the is causing extra instructions to be generated or maybe the compiler can
calculate the offset/address to the nested variable and there is no performance
consequence. I kinda hope there as adding a "context" will likely cost a extra
copy. Even if it does I'm willing to take the "one-time" hit rather than seeing
the `args.repo.repo` and the like!

Thoughts

aiTitle: Refactor nested CLI args to reduce redundancy

input.command: ls src/options_flags/ && echo "---" && grep -n "args\." src/init.rs | head -40
input.description: Look at options_flags layout and args usage

content.content:
1	//! `--account` — selects an account section in the user config.
2	//! See [options_flags](README.md) for shared architecture.
3	
4	use clap::Args;
5	
6	/// `--account` leaf (Option — non-boolean domain) — see
7	/// [Consuming an OF](README.md#consuming-an-of).
8	#[derive(Args, Debug, Clone, Default)]
9	pub struct AccountOption {
10	    /// Account name — picks `[account.<a>]` from user config.
11	    ///
12	    /// - Without this flag, `[default].account` (or top-level
13	    ///   `[repo]` shorthand) is used.
14	    /// - Meaningful only with Path or bare-NAME targets — URL /
15	    ///   owner/name targets supply the remote directly.
16	    #[arg(long, value_name = "NAME", verbatim_doc_comment)]
17	    pub account: Option<String>,
18	}
19

1	# options_flags — Reusable CLI Options and Flags
2	
3	Per-flag CLI surfaces shared across multiple subcommands. Each
4	shared option or flag (OF) lives in its own submodule so its
5	type, parser, and tests stay together.
6	
7	## Architecture
8	
9	Three composition patterns:
10	
11	- **Leaf** — a `#[derive(Args)]` struct holding the flag(s),
12	  `value_parser`, default, and help text (via the field
13	  doc-comment). One `#[derive(Args)]` per logical OF — single
14	  flag or a small fixed pair (e.g. `push_retry`'s two fields).
15	  Help text aims to be generic enough for any reasonable
16	  consumer.
17	- **Bundle** — a `#[derive(Args)]` that flattens N leaves into a
18	  named role (e.g. `ProvisionOptionFlagBundle`). One
19	  `#[command(flatten)]` line at the consumer picks up the whole
20	  bundle.
21	- **Pattern A escape hatch** — when a consumer needs unique help
22	  text (or different defaults), it skips the leaf's flatten and
23	  inlines its own `#[arg(value_parser = …)]` field, reusing the
24	  leaf's typed value and parser.
25	
26	## Flag vs Option — classify by domain, not wire syntax
27	
28	Every leaf falls into one of two categories, picked by the value
29	domain it carries:
30	
31	- **Flag** — boolean domain. Wire form may be presence/absence
32	  (`--dry-run`), explicit negation (`--no-dry-run`), or value
33	  (`--dry-run=true|false`). All three express a `bool`. Type
34	  name suffix: `*Flag`.
35	- **Option** — non-boolean domain (string, integer, enum, path,
36	  …). Wire form is always `--name=<value>` (or `--name <value>`).
37	  Type name suffix: `*Option` (or `*Options` for a multi-field
38	  leaf like `PushRetryOptions`).
39	
40	Wire form is presentation; the category is the underlying domain.
41	
42	## Adding a new leaf
43	
44	1. Create `options_flags/<name>.rs` with a
45	   `#[derive(Args, Debug, Clone, Default)]` struct.
46	2. Pick the suffix per domain: `*Flag` (boolean) or `*Option(s)`
47	   (non-boolean).
48	3. Add `pub mod <name>;` to `mod.rs`.
49	4. If the leaf has explicit parsing logic, declare a unit-struct
50	   implementor of `FlagParser` (boolean domain) or `OptionParser`
51	   (non-boolean domain). Bare `Option<String>` leaves and
52	   presence/absence boolean leaves need no parser impl — clap
53	   handles them.
54	5. Add tests for any non-trivial parser/resolver logic.
55	6. Leaves do **not** implement a Bundle marker — bundles do.
56	
57	## Consuming an OF
58	
59	Default — flatten the leaf into your subcommand's `Args`:
60	
61	```rust
62	#[derive(Args)]
63	pub struct MyArgs {
64	    #[command(flatten)]
65	    pub config: ConfigOption,
66	    // ...
67	}
68	```
69	
70	Pattern A — when generic help doesn't fit:
71	
72	```rust
73	#[derive(Args)]
74	pub struct MyArgs {
75	    /// My subcommand-specific help for --config.
76	    #[arg(long = "config", value_name = "none|PATH",
77	          verbatim_doc_comment)]
78	    pub config: Option<String>,
79	    // ...
80	}
81	```
82	
83	A Pattern A consumer reuses the leaf's types (e.g. `ConfigKind`)
84	and parsers (e.g. `parse_config_kind`) but owns its own clap
85	attributes.
86	
87	## Consumer function shape
88	
89	Helper functions called by a subcommand body should accept the
90	relevant leaf type by reference rather than unpacking individual
91	fields at the call site:
92	
93	```rust
94	// Multi-field leaf — pass the whole leaf
95	fn run_retry(cmd: &str, args: &[&str], cwd: &Path,
96	             retry: &PushRetryOptions) -> Result<…> { … }
97	
98	run_retry("git", &["push", …], cwd, &args.provision.push_retry)?;
99	```
100	
101	This wins on:
102	- Readability — `&args.provision.push_retry` reads as a single
103	  unit; the function body works with `retry.push_retries` (no
104	  leaf-name doubling because the parameter name is the consumer's
105	  choice).
106	- Argument count — every multi-field leaf collapses N args into
107	  one ref, so chained helpers don't accumulate
108	  `clippy::too_many_arguments` warnings.
109	- Future-proofing — extending a leaf with another field is a
110	  zero-touch change at every call site.
111	
112	For **single-field leaves** (e.g. `DryRunFlag`, `PrivateFlag`),
113	direct read at the consumer site (`args.provision.dry_run.dry_run`)
114	is fine — wrapping a `bool` in `&LeafType` parameter doesn't earn
115	the indirection.
116	
117	## Marker traits
118	
119	Three Bundle markers — one per content classification. Bundles
120	implement exactly one. Leaves do **not** implement these markers
121	(their category lives in the type-name suffix).
122	
123	- `FlagBundle: clap::Args` — pure-boolean bundle (every
124	  constituent leaf is a Flag). Rare in practice.
125	- `OptionBundle: clap::Args` — pure-non-boolean bundle (every
126	  constituent leaf is an Option).
127	- `OptionFlagBundle: clap::Args` — mixed bundle (constituents
128	  include both Flag and Option leaves). Most common in practice.
129	
130	Two Parser traits — conditional contracts implemented only when
131	a leaf has explicit parsing logic (custom `value_parser`):
132	
133	- `FlagParser` — boolean-domain parser. Implemented only for
134	  boolean leaves that take a value form (`--flag=true|false`).
135	  Presence/absence flags need no impl; clap parses directly.
136	- `OptionParser` — non-boolean-domain parser. Implemented only
137	  for leaves with custom parsing logic (e.g. `ScopeParser`,
138	  `RepoParser`); bare `Option<String>` leaves need no impl.
139	
140	`FlagParser` and `OptionParser` share an identical method shape;
141	the difference is documentation (which value-domain the parser
142	targets).
143	
144	All five traits are documentation-level — clap derive doesn't
145	see them; the discipline is that every leaf and bundle declares
146	the appropriate ones explicitly.
147	
148	## Bundle marker discipline
149	
150	Bundle markers aren't compiler-checked against contents. If a
151	bundle's leaf set shifts category — e.g. a pure-Flag bundle gains
152	an Option leaf — you must update the marker by hand
153	(`FlagBundle` → `OptionFlagBundle`). No lint, no failure; silent
154	drift if forgotten. Worth a glance at the marker line whenever a
155	bundle's `#[command(flatten)]` set changes.
156	
157	## Pattern A worked example
158	
159	`init`'s `--config` field originally carried init-specific help
160	("Only valid with `--scope=por`..."). It currently flattens
161	`ConfigOption` for the generic help; the `--scope=por` constraint
162	surfaces in `init`'s preflight error. If the help-text generality
163	becomes a usability problem, init will switch to Pattern A as the
164	worked example.
165	
166	## Layout note
167	
168	OFs currently sit as flat `<name>.rs` files alongside this
169	README. Bundle modules carry the `_bundle` suffix
170	(e.g. `provision_bundle.rs`) so the leaf/bundle split is visible
171	in the directory listing without opening files. If an individual
172	OF accumulates enough rationale, edge-case detail, or examples
173	to outgrow doc-comments, it will graduate to a `<name>/mod.rs` +
174	`<name>/README.md` subdirectory layout. Mechanical when needed;
175	not done preemptively.

file.content:
# options_flags — Reusable CLI Options and Flags

Per-flag CLI surfaces shared across multiple subcommands. Each
shared option or flag (OF) lives in its own submodule so its
type, parser, and tests stay together.

## Architecture

Three composition patterns:

- **Leaf** — a `#[derive(Args)]` struct holding the flag(s),
  `value_parser`, default, and help text (via the field
  doc-comment). One `#[derive(Args)]` per logical OF — single
  flag or a small fixed pair (e.g. `push_retry`'s two fields).
  Help text aims to be generic enough for any reasonable
  consumer.
- **Bundle** — a `#[derive(Args)]` that flattens N leaves into a
  named role (e.g. `ProvisionOptionFlagBundle`). One
  `#[command(flatten)]` line at the consumer picks up the whole
  bundle.
- **Pattern A escape hatch** — when a consumer needs unique help
  text (or different defaults), it skips the leaf's flatten and
  inlines its own `#[arg(value_parser = …)]` field, reusing the
  leaf's typed value and parser.

## Flag vs Option — classify by domain, not wire syntax

Every leaf falls into one of two categories, picked by the value
domain it carries:

- **Flag** — boolean domain. Wire form may be presence/absence
  (`--dry-run`), explicit negation (`--no-dry-run`), or value
  (`--dry-run=true|false`). All three express a `bool`. Type
  name suffix: `*Flag`.
- **Option** — non-boolean domain (string, integer, enum, path,
  …). Wire form is always `--name=<value>` (or `--name <value>`).
  Type name suffix: `*Option` (or `*Options` for a multi-field
  leaf like `PushRetryOptions`).

Wire form is presentation; the category is the underlying domain.

## Adding a new leaf

1. Create `options_flags/<name>.rs` with a
   `#[derive(Args, Debug, Clone, Default)]` struct.
2. Pick the suffix per domain: `*Flag` (boolean) or `*Option(s)`
   (non-boolean).
3. Add `pub mod <name>;` to `mod.rs`.
4. If the leaf has explicit parsing logic, declare a unit-struct
   implementor of `FlagParser` (boolean domain) or `OptionParser`
   (non-boolean domain). Bare `Option<String>` leaves and
   presence/absence boolean leaves need no parser impl — clap
   handles them.
5. Add tests for any non-trivial parser/resolver logic.
6. Leaves do **not** implement a Bundle marker — bundles do.

## Consuming an OF

Default — flatten the leaf into your subcommand's `Args`:

```rust
#[derive(Args)]
pub struct MyArgs {
    #[command(flatten)]
    pub config: ConfigOption,
    // ...
}
```

Pattern A — when generic help doesn't fit:

```rust
#[derive(Args)]
pub struct MyArgs {
    /// My subcommand-specific help for --config.
    #[arg(long = "config", value_name = "none|PATH",
          verbatim_doc_comment)]
    pub config: Option<String>,
    // ...
}
```

A Pattern A consumer reuses the leaf's types (e.g. `ConfigKind`)
and parsers (e.g. `parse_config_kind`) but owns its own clap
attributes.

## Consumer function shape

Helper functions called by a subcommand body should accept the
relevant leaf type by reference rather than unpacking individual
fields at the call site:

```rust
// Multi-field leaf — pass the whole leaf
fn run_retry(cmd: &str, args: &[&str], cwd: &Path,
             retry: &PushRetryOptions) -> Result<…> { … }

run_retry("git", &["push", …], cwd, &args.provision.push_retry)?;
```

This wins on:
- Readability — `&args.provision.push_retry` reads as a single
  unit; the function body works with `retry.push_retries` (no
  leaf-name doubling because the parameter name is the consumer's
  choice).
- Argument count — every multi-field leaf collapses N args into
  one ref, so chained helpers don't accumulate
  `clippy::too_many_arguments` warnings.
- Future-proofing — extending a leaf with another field is a
  zero-touch change at every call site.

For **single-field leaves** (e.g. `DryRunFlag`, `PrivateFlag`),
direct read at the consumer site (`args.provision.dry_run.dry_run`)
is fine — wrapping a `bool` in `&LeafType` parameter doesn't earn
the indirection.

## Marker traits

Three Bundle markers — one per content classification. Bundles
implement exactly one. Leaves do **not** implement these markers
(their category lives in the type-name suffix).

- `FlagBundle: clap::Args` — pure-boolean bundle (every
  constituent leaf is a Flag). Rare in practice.
- `OptionBundle: clap::Args` — pure-non-boolean bundle (every
  constituent leaf is an Option).
- `OptionFlagBundle: clap::Args` — mixed bundle (constituents
  include both Flag and Option leaves). Most common in practice.

Two Parser traits — conditional contracts implemented only when
a leaf has explicit parsing logic (custom `value_parser`):

- `FlagParser` — boolean-domain parser. Implemented only for
  boolean leaves that take a value form (`--flag=true|false`).
  Presence/absence flags need no impl; clap parses directly.
- `OptionParser` — non-boolean-domain parser. Implemented only
  for leaves with custom parsing logic (e.g. `ScopeParser`,
  `RepoParser`); bare `Option<String>` leaves need no impl.

`FlagParser` and `OptionParser` share an identical method shape;
the difference is documentation (which value-domain the parser
targets).

All five traits are documentation-level — clap derive doesn't
see them; the discipline is that every leaf and bundle declares
the appropriate ones explicitly.

## Bundle marker discipline

Bundle markers aren't compiler-checked against contents. If a
bundle's leaf set shifts category — e.g. a pure-Flag bundle gains
an Option leaf — you must update the marker by hand
(`FlagBundle` → `OptionFlagBundle`). No lint, no failure; silent
drift if forgotten. Worth a glance at the marker line whenever a
bundle's `#[command(flatten)]` set changes.

## Pattern A worked example

`init`'s `--config` field originally carried init-specific help
("Only valid with `--scope=por`..."). It currently flattens
`ConfigOption` for the generic help; the `--scope=por` constraint
surfaces in `init`'s preflight error. If the help-text generality
becomes a usability problem, init will switch to Pattern A as the
worked example.

## Layout note

OFs currently sit as flat `<name>.rs` files alongside this
README. Bundle modules carry the `_bundle` suffix
(e.g. `provision_bundle.rs`) so the leaf/bundle split is visible
in the directory listing without opening files. If an individual
OF accumulates enough rationale, edge-case detail, or examples
to outgrow doc-comments, it will graduate to a `<name>/mod.rs` +
`<name>/README.md` subdirectory layout. Mechanical when needed;
not done preemptively.

aiTitle: Refactor nested CLI args to reduce redundancy

content.text:
A few observations before recommending.

**Performance: no cost.** `args.account.account` compiles to the same instruction as a flat `args.account` would — a single load with a statically-known offset added. Rust struct layout offsets are known at compile time, and nested field projection is just `base + offset_outer + offset_inner`, summed by the compiler. The doubling is a readability problem, not a runtime one. So a "context" struct that clones values is *strictly worse* on perf (one-time but real String clones at the boundary), even if it's cheap enough to ignore.

**Where the awkwardness comes from.** Each leaf struct names its single field after the long flag (`AccountOption.account`, `RepoOption.repo`, `ScopeOption.scope`, `PrivateFlag.private`, …). Then the consumer flattens it under the same wrapper name. So you get `args.account.account` purely because both layers chose the same identifier.

**Three options, roughly in order of cost vs. payoff:**

1. **Rename the inner field on each leaf** (cheapest, biggest visual win).
   Override clap's flag derivation with `#[arg(long = "account", …)]` and call the field something like `value` / `selector` / `kind` / `set`. Then:
   ```
   args.account.account  →  args.account.value
   args.repo.repo        →  args.repo.selector
   args.scope.scope      →  args.scope.kind
   args.provision.private.private →  args.provision.private.set
   ```
   Still nested, but each level *adds* information instead of repeating. Mechanical rename, no architectural change, no copies.

2. **Accessor methods on `InitArgs`** for the hot paths.
   ```rust
   impl InitArgs {
       fn account(&self) -> Option<&str> { self.account.account.as_deref() }
       fn scope(&self) -> ScopeKind { self.scope.scope }
       fn private(&self) -> bool { self.provision.private.private }
       // ...
   }
   ```
   Inlined in release — same machine code as direct field access. Body reads `args.account()` / `args.scope()`. Pairs well with (1) and is per-consumer (each subcommand picks its own accessor surface).

3. **Context struct** (your idea). A flat `InitContext` built once at the top of `plan_init`:
   ```rust
   struct InitContext { target: String, account: Option<String>, repo: Option<RepoSelector>, scope: ScopeKind, private: bool, dry_run: bool, ... }
   impl From<&InitArgs> for InitContext { ... }
   ```
   Cleanest body, but you pay a few `String`/`Option<String>` clones once at the boundary. As you said, that's a one-time hit and probably negligible — but it's also the only option of the three that has *any* runtime cost. And it duplicates the schema in two places (clap struct + context struct), so adding a new flag is a two-edit change.

**My recommendation: (1) + (2).** Rename the inner leaf fields so each segment contributes meaning, then add accessor methods on the consumer args struct for the values used in many places. Zero copies, no schema duplication, and the README's stated rule "pass `&args.provision.push_retry` rather than unpacking" still holds.

If you do want (3) anyway because a flat `InitContext` has documentation/typing value of its own (e.g. `plan_init` should not see clap types at all — which is a real argument for layering), that's fine too — but I'd reach for it as a deliberate "decouple plan from CLI" move, not just to fix the ergonomic itch.

Want me to spike option (1) on one leaf (e.g. `AccountOption`) so you can see the diff before committing to a sweep?

message.content: Not a bad solution, but I taking the cost up front and decoupling cli arguments from the subcommands, I see the possibility this code being using in a GUI where clap wouldn't be used, I don't think, and thus it would need a refactor at that point. Is that a truthful argument?

Not a bad solution, but I taking the cost up front and decoupling cli arguments from the subcommands, I see the possibility this code being using in a GUI where clap wouldn't be used, I don't think, and thus it would need a refactor at that point. Is that a truthful argument?

Not a bad solution, but I'd rather take the cost up front and decoupling cli arguments from the subcommands. I see the possibility that this code could be used in a GUI where clap wouldn't be used, I don't think, and thus it would need a refactor at that point. Is that a truthful argument?

content.text:
Partially truthful, but it's the weakest of the available justifications.

**Where it's true:** `plan_init(args: &InitArgs, ...)` does take a clap-derived type, so any non-clap caller (GUI, library embedding, scripted batch driver) has to either construct `InitArgs` directly — which means depending on clap and filling in clap-shaped wrappers — or you do the refactor then. So yes, there's real coupling, and a context struct decouples it.

**Where it's weak:**

1. **No GUI is on the roadmap.** Designing for a hypothetical front-end is the exact "design for future requirements" pattern CLAUDE.md tells you to avoid. If/when a GUI shows up, introducing `InitContext` + `From<&InitArgs>` is mechanical — the cost of doing it later is roughly the cost of doing it now, so the speculative case doesn't add urgency.

2. **The clap types are already pretty thin.** Most leaves wrap `Option<String>`, `Option<RepoSelector>`, `bool`, or `u32`. A non-CLI caller could fill in `InitArgs { account: AccountOption { account: Some("...".into()) }, ... }` mechanically — annoying, not blocking. So the GUI's *actual* pain point is the verbosity, not a hard wall.

3. **The honest motivation is current, not future.** The real wins of `InitContext` are present-tense:
   - Subcommand bodies read flat (`ctx.account` not `args.account.account`) — your original complaint.
   - Tests construct a context with named fields directly, no nested clap wrappers.
   - One explicit type documents "this is what `plan_init` consumes," instead of that surface being implicit in the clap-flatten ordering of `InitArgs`.

So the *truthful* version of your argument is: "I want flat field access in subcommand bodies, and I want the subcommand layer to declare its inputs explicitly rather than inheriting clap's shape. The decoupling is a side benefit that pays off if a non-CLI front-end ever appears." That framing matches the actual code change and survives YAGNI scrutiny — the GUI is bonus, not justification.

If you lead with "future GUI" in the chore notes, a future reader (or future-you) will reasonably ask "did a GUI ever appear?" and conclude the refactor was speculative. Lead with readability + testability; mention GUI decoupling as a downstream consequence.

So if we were starting a new project which architecture would you advice?

You're right, vc-x1 may never have a GUI, but one of the main objectives of this code derivitives of this code will explore the boi conversation and the generated code, that is going to require a TUI/GUI to support a multi-window solution. But "I want flat file access ..." is the obvous one and hiding it behind renaming and/or accessors feels like hiding a architectural mistake. And this code refactoring is to define a "clean" architecture for future tasks outside of this project.

So if we were starting a new project which architecture would you advise?

content.text:
For a new project where multiple front-ends are plausible, the architecture I'd recommend is the standard "thin CLI adapter over a clap-free core" — same shape Cargo and rust-analyzer use. Concretely:

**Layering**

```
src/
  lib.rs         // public API surface
  ops/
    init.rs      // pub fn init(opts: &InitOptions, ...) -> Result<InitOutcome>
    clone.rs
    sync.rs
    ...
  bin/
    cli.rs       // clap parsing only; converts to ops types and calls them
```

The library knows nothing about clap. The binary is the only place clap exists.

**Five rules that make this work**

1. **Options are plain Rust types, flat-fielded, with `Default`.** `InitOptions { target: String, account: Option<String>, repo: Option<RepoSelector>, scope: ScopeKind, private: bool, ... }`. No nesting unless the nesting carries domain meaning (`RepoSelector` is fine — it's a domain value; `RepoOption` wrapping `Option<RepoSelector>` is not, that's pure clap scaffolding). A GUI/TUI builds these from form state; a CLI builds them from `InitArgs`.

2. **Errors are typed at the API boundary, not `Box<dyn Error>`.** `enum InitError { ... }` (often via `thiserror`). A CLI can `Display` them; a GUI can match on the variant to pop the right dialog ("repo already exists — overwrite?"). `Box<dyn Error>` throws that information away exactly when a GUI most needs it.

3. **Outputs are values, not `println!`.** Each op returns a structured result (`InitOutcome { created_paths: Vec<PathBuf>, pushed_bookmarks: Vec<...>, ... }`). The CLI formats it for stdout; the GUI populates a panel. The library never writes to stdout itself.

4. **Progress goes through a sink, not stderr.** For long-running ops (sync, push, clone), accept an optional `&mut dyn ProgressSink` (or an `mpsc::Sender<Event>`). CLI installs a sink that writes log lines; TUI installs one that updates a status bar; tests install a recording sink. `log`/`tracing` is fine for diagnostics, but it's lossy for structured progress — a GUI will want both.

5. **No globals, no implicit cwd/env reads in ops.** Anything an op needs (workspace root, user config, env-derived paths) goes in the options or a `Workspace` handle passed in. The CLI resolves cwd/env once at startup and constructs the handle. This is what makes a multi-window TUI feasible — different windows can drive different workspaces with no cross-contamination.

**Crate split: defer.**

Same-crate `mod` separation (with `lib.rs` reachable to multiple binaries) is enough until the GUI lands in a separate repo. Splitting into `vc-x1-core` + `vc-x1-cli` early adds friction (workspace `Cargo.toml`, version coupling, re-exports) without buying anything you don't already get from module discipline. Promote to a workspace when there's a second consumer.

**For vc-x1 specifically**

The refactor that aligns with this is what you've already gravitated toward: introduce `InitOptions`/`CloneOptions`/etc. as plain structs, port `plan_init` and friends to take them, and keep `InitArgs` as a clap-only adapter at the binary edge with `From<&InitArgs> for InitOptions` (or a `to_options()` method). Errors and outputs are partly there already — `Box<dyn Error>` is the next thing to typed-up if a TUI is in your future. Progress sinks can wait until the first long op grows a real progress story; just leave room for the parameter.

The one cost worth naming: every new flag is now a two-edit change (clap struct + options struct + the `From` impl). That's the price of decoupling. In return, your subcommand bodies stay flat and your test setups stop constructing nested clap wrappers.

message.content: Well stated, capture this in a chores design note. Is thei "options or Workspace handle" what I call "context"? What I'm thinking is each and every subcommand would get the same context even if they don't need all of it's information, is that rational good practice or are there better solutions?

Well stated, capture this in a chores design note. Is the "options or Workspace handle" what I call "context"? What I'm thinking is each and every subcommand would get the same context even if they don't need all of it's information, is that good practice or are there better solutions?

content.text:
Before writing the note, let me answer the question — it affects the note's content.

**"Workspace handle" vs your "context"**

I named two distinct things, and they're worth keeping distinct:

- **Per-op Options** (`InitOptions`, `CloneOptions`, …) — shape differs per subcommand; describes *what this specific operation does*.
- **Workspace handle** — shared environment: workspace root, loaded user config, progress sink, maybe a logger. Same shape across all subcommands.

Your "context" sounds like the second — the shared environment. So the function shape becomes:

```rust
fn init(ws: &Workspace, opts: &InitOptions) -> Result<InitOutcome, InitError>
```

That's two parameters, not one merged context. The split is meaningful: `Workspace` is the *platform* you're running against; `Options` is *what to do on it*.

**Is one uniform context to every subcommand good practice?**

A genuine `Workspace` handle, yes — every op needs the workspace root and the user config, so passing it everywhere isn't false uniformity, it's truth. A "god context" with every possible dependency in one struct, no — that's an antipattern even if it looks tidy:

- The signature stops telling you what the function depends on. Tests have to build the whole thing.
- Adding a field perturbs every caller's mental model even though most don't use it.
- Hidden coupling: you can't tell from outside which subcommand reads which field.

The healthy line is "shared cross-cutting environment goes in `Workspace`; everything else is op-specific." When you find yourself adding a field to `Workspace` because exactly one subcommand needs it — that field belongs in that subcommand's `Options` instead.

A few escape valves if `Workspace` itself grows:

- Split it: `Workspace` (paths + config, read-only, cheap to clone) vs. `Session` (mutable progress sink, cancellation token). Most ops want `&Workspace`; long-running ops also want `&mut Session` or a `&Session` with interior mutability.
- If the day comes that different ops need genuinely different platform surfaces, traits (`fn init<W: HasConfig + HasFs>(ws: &W, ...)`) — but defer; this is heavy in Rust and usually not worth it.

**Note placement**

Two reasonable homes:

1. New section in `notes/chores-09.md` (current chores file).
2. New `notes/chores-10.md` opened for forward-looking architecture, since this isn't tied to a specific 0.42.0-N step — it's a design target the rest of the cycle (and follow-on cycles) will work toward.

I'd lean (2) — chores-09 is the close-out of `init-clone-refactor-rebase-landing` and tagging an unrelated forward design onto it muddles the section. (1) is fine if you'd rather not start a new file yet.

Either way I'd add a corresponding todo entry pointing at the section so it's discoverable.

Which placement do you want, and any tweaks to the framing above before I write it?

message.content: Agreed, Workspace handle == Context"

Ok WorkspaceHandle == Context, It's also true each subcommand has an explicit set of arguments. My thinking is that every individual arugment is defined in src/options_flags and then each subcommand "bundles" those arguments in what it needs so that `vc-x1 subcmd -h` is specific to each subcommand. But I don't want those parameters accessed with args.xxx.xxx, if possible!

Another thing I want is command completion, so `vc-x1 init <TAB>` needs to work, I suspect that the answer to that question will help guide us to the "best" solution.

content.text:
Completion doesn't change the answer — it confirms it.

**Why completion needs the clap struct unchanged**

`clap_complete` (and especially `unstable-dynamic`, which you already have) reads structure from clap's derive metadata: `Args::augment_args`, `CommandFactory::command()`, attached `value_parser`s, value hints, and `ArgValueCompleter`s. That metadata lives on the `#[derive(Args)]` types — `InitArgs` and the leaves in `src/options_flags/`. Whatever architecture we pick, those have to stay. Dynamic-value completion (`--account=<TAB>` against accounts in user config) is also implemented at this layer via `ArgValueCompleter` attached to the leaf — that's a clap-only concern that has no business leaking into ops.

So completion *forces* the clap-typed `InitArgs` to exist with its leaves and bundles. It doesn't force ops to consume `InitArgs`.

**The shape that satisfies all four constraints**

```
shell completion ← clap metadata ← InitArgs  (leaves flattened, drives -h)
                                     │
                                     │ From<&InitArgs> for InitOptions
                                     ▼
                                  InitOptions  (plain, flat, no clap)
                                     │
                                     ▼
                              ops::init(ws: &Workspace, opts: &InitOptions)
```

What this gives you:

- **Per-subcommand `-h`** — `InitArgs` flattens exactly the leaves init needs. `clone -h`, `sync -h` show only their own bundles. Unchanged from today.
- **Tab completion** — clap derive on `InitArgs`/leaves powers it, including dynamic completers attached to leaves.
- **No `args.xxx.xxx` in op bodies** — bodies see `opts.account`, `opts.scope`, `opts.private` as flat fields on `InitOptions`.
- **Leaf reuse** — `src/options_flags/` is unchanged; each leaf still defines flag, parser, help text, completer once.

**The one place the nesting still appears**

The `From<&InitArgs> for InitOptions` impl walks the nesting once:

```rust
impl From<&InitArgs> for InitOptions {
    fn from(a: &InitArgs) -> Self {
        Self {
            target: a.target.clone(),
            account: a.account.account.clone(),
            repo: a.repo.repo.clone(),
            scope: a.scope.scope,
            private: a.provision.private.private,
            // ...
        }
    }
}
```

Ugly but contained — one site per subcommand. After this hop, every ops body is flat. Since you've already accepted the one-time conversion cost, this is the right place to spend it.

**Why this is better than the alternatives**

- *Single flat struct, no clap layer:* loses completion and `--help` formatting. Non-starter.
- *Inline every arg per subcommand (Pattern A everywhere):* loses leaf reuse. The shared options_flags/ architecture goes away.
- *Macro-generated accessors hiding the nesting:* hides the wart instead of fixing it. You said you don't want that, and I agree — it papers over the actual architecture (clap layer + ops layer) instead of expressing it.

**For the chores note**

I'd still lean `notes/chores-10.md` — this is forward-looking architecture spanning the rest of 0.42.0 and beyond. I'll capture:

1. Goals (decouple ops from CLI; flat field access in bodies; preserve completion + per-subcommand help; reusable leaves).
2. The two-layer shape (`InitArgs` clap-side / `InitOptions` ops-side / `Workspace` shared handle).
3. The five rules (plain options, typed errors, returned outcomes, progress sinks, no implicit env reads).
4. Where completion lives (clap layer; dynamic completers on leaves).
5. The contained-conversion wart and why it's acceptable.
6. What it's *not*: god context, premature trait-based DI, premature crate split.
7. Migration sketch: which subcommand to convert first as the worked example.

Confirm `chores-10.md` and I'll write it. Or if you'd prefer one section in `chores-09.md`, say the word.

content: Goal: define a clean two-layer architecture (clap InitArgs → plain InitOptions + shared Workspace handle) so ops bodies get flat field access while completion and per-subcommand help still work. Next: confirm chores-10.md as the file and I'll write the design note. (disable recaps in /config)

# Chores-09.md

General chores notes — design captures (forward-looking) and
post-implementation chore entries. Same shape as chores-01..08.md;
09 starts here because chores-07 (0.42.0 cycle, 600+ lines) and
chores-08 (0.41.1 cycle, 1500+ lines) are both already large; the
init-clone-refactor rebase landing is a natural new-file
boundary.

Subsection headers use the trailing-version format from CLAUDE.md
when they correspond to a release: `## Description (X.Y.Z)`.

## init-clone-refactor rebase landing (0.42.0-4.7)

Rebased main (0.42.0-0..-4.6) onto init-clone-refactor at its
0.41.1 close-out tip (`slvprlpw`). Chids preserved; ochid
trailers in the rebased commits stayed structurally valid (the
.claude pairings they point at had been WC-only during the icr
divergence; -4.7 itself re-establishes the .claude side).

### What needed real work

- **ttzwvpoq (0.42.0-1):** full `Scope` enum lifted wholesale
  from pre-rebase main; `#[allow(dead_code)]` on `is_bot_only`
  and `Single` / `parse_scope` since icr's bundle design keeps
  them dormant.
- **mvusyowm (0.42.0-2):** icr's bundle-flatten `InitArgs` +
  `ScopeOption`/`ScopeKind` already supersedes mvusyowm's
  parser-switch + `Option<Scope>` design; took icr wholesale
  across all 5 init.rs conflict regions. The cycle's narrative
  intent (custom `--scope` parser accepting paths) becomes
  dormant on the icr base; the `Scope::Single` variant survives
  for future use.
- **kyurxpnu (0.42.0-4.5):** CLAUDE.md substep references merged
  (lulqxovr's icr-side decomposition pointer + kyurxpnu's
  external `substep-protocol.md` / `jj-revsets.md` links) into
  a single continuation paragraph under `### Versioning`.
- **Reference renumbering** — cycle's `[72]`/`[73]`/`[74]`
  collided with icr's chores-08 anchors: `[72]→[76]`,
  `[73]→[77]`, `[74]→[78]`, plus self-refs in chores-07's
  -4.5 and -4.6 sections.

### Precautions taken (most unnecessary in retrospect)

- `main-2` local duplicate bookmark — never read.
- `gca-icr-main` common-ancestor marker — never referenced.
- `rslv-commit` cursor bookmark — handy as a navigation
  aid during cascade, not load-bearing (`@-` works).
- `../vc-x1-main` + `../vc-x1-icr` reference clones — useful
  for content lookup at specific chids; replaceable with
  `jj log -r <chid> --patch` against the local repo.
- Filesystem snapshots `vc-x1-20260505-1`, `vc-x1-20260506-1`
  — never restored from.
- `~/vc-x1-rebase-status.md` scratch file — useful for
  resuming across sessions, but ~200 lines was over-detailed;
  a 30-line cursor + decisions log would have sufficed.

### What was load-bearing

- `jj op restore` for local rewinds.
- origin's canonical pre-rebase main remaining intact until
  the explicit force-push at the end (the only fallback that
  mattered).
- jj's chid-preservation across rebase: ochid trailers stay
  valid pointers without manual fixup.
- Per-commit cargo cycle (fmt/clippy/test/install --locked)
  to verify each squash kept the chain buildable.

### Distilled recipe for next time

1. `jj rebase` of the divergent chain onto the new base.
2. Per conflicted commit: `jj new <commit>`, edit conflicts,
   cargo cycle, `jj squash`.
3. After cascade clears: cargo cycle at tip, `jj git push
   --bookmark main`.
4. `.claude` side: `vc-x1 push` to author the paired commit
   once main converges with origin.

Skip the duplicate bookmarks, reference clones, and
filesystem snapshots unless a specific reason emerges. The
op-log + origin are the safety net.

### Edits

- `notes/chores-09.md`: this file (new).
- `notes/todo.md`: ladder marker flips
  (-4.6 (current)→(done), add -4.7 (current)); add `-4.7`
  Done entry referenced as `[79]`; add `[79]:` ref target;
  move pre-0.42.0 Done entries to `notes/done.md`.
- `notes/done.md`: append migrated 0.40/0.41 Done entries.
- `Cargo.toml`: bump 0.42.0-4.6 → 0.42.0-4.7.

- `notes/done.md`: append migrated 0.40/0.41 Done entries.
- `Cargo.toml`: bump 0.42.0-4.6 → 0.42.0-4.7.

## Ops layer architecture (forward-looking)

Design target for subsequent cycles: separate clap-aware
parsing from subcommand operation logic so future front-ends
(e.g. a TUI for bot-conversation exploration, or library
embedding) call the same core without retrofitting. Captures
the conclusion of the 0.42.0-4.7 side discussion about
`args.account.account` ergonomics — renaming / accessor
shortcuts were rejected as hiding the architecture mismatch
rather than fixing it.

### Goals

- Subcommand bodies access flat fields (`opts.account`), not
  nested clap shapes (`args.account.account`).
- `src/options_flags/` leaves remain the single source of
  truth for flag definition (parser, help, completer).
- Per-subcommand `-h` stays bundle-specific.
- Tab completion (`vc-x1 init <TAB>`, `--account=<TAB>`)
  keeps working via clap_complete + dynamic completers.
- The ops layer is callable without clap as a dependency
  (today's `plan_init(args: &InitArgs, ...)` is not).

### Two layers, three structures per subcommand

- **CLI layer (clap-aware):** `InitArgs` / `CloneArgs` / etc.
  `#[derive(Args)]` types that flatten leaves from
  `src/options_flags/`. Own clap metadata for `-h` and
  completion. Live at the binary edge.
- **Ops layer (clap-free):** `InitOptions` / `CloneOptions`
  — plain structs, flat fields, `Default`. Plus `Workspace`,
  the shared platform handle (workspace root, loaded
  `UserConfig`, optional progress sink). Entrypoints are
  `ops::init(ws: &Workspace, opts: &InitOptions) -> Result<InitOutcome, InitError>`.
- **Boundary conversion:** `From<&InitArgs> for InitOptions`
  — one contained site per subcommand where leaf nesting is
  unpacked. Op bodies never see `args.xxx.xxx`.

### `Workspace` ("context") vs per-op options

- **`Workspace`:** shared platform every op runs against —
  workspace root, loaded user config, optional progress
  sink. Same shape across subcommands.
- **`InitOptions`** etc.: per-op input; shape differs by
  subcommand.

Two parameters, not a single merged "god context." The
signature `fn init(ws: &Workspace, opts: &InitOptions)`
documents what the op depends on; a merged `Context`
containing every possible field would let any op silently
read any field with no signature-level visibility.

If `Workspace` itself grows, escape valves (defer until
needed):

- Split into `Workspace` (paths + config, read-only, cheap)
  vs `Session` (progress / cancellation, mutable). Most ops
  want `&Workspace`; long-running ops also accept
  `&Session`.
- Trait-based DI (`fn init<W: HasConfig + HasFs>(...)`) for
  multiple front-ends with genuinely different platform
  surfaces. Heavy in Rust; not the default.

### Five rules for the ops layer

1. **Plain options, flat fields, `Default`.** No clap types
   in `InitOptions`. Domain values like `RepoSelector` are
   fine (they're domain types); leaf wrappers like
   `RepoOption` are not.
2. **Typed errors.** `enum InitError` (likely via
   `thiserror`), not `Box<dyn Error>`. A TUI matches
   variants to pick dialogs; CLI formats them.
   `Box<dyn Error>` discards exactly the information a GUI
   most needs.
3. **Returned outcomes, not `println!`.** Each op returns a
   structured result; CLI formats it for stdout, TUI
   populates panels. Library writes nothing to stdout
   itself.
4. **Progress via a sink.** Long-running ops accept an
   optional `&mut dyn ProgressSink` (or
   `mpsc::Sender<Event>`). CLI installs a stderr sink; TUI
   a status-bar sink; tests a recording sink. `log` /
   `tracing` covers diagnostics but is lossy for structured
   progress.
5. **No globals, no implicit cwd/env reads in ops.**
   Everything an op needs goes in `Workspace` or
   `*Options`. CLI resolves cwd/env once at startup and
   builds the handle. This is what lets a multi-window TUI
   drive multiple workspaces without cross-contamination.

### Completion stays at the clap layer

- Static structure (`-h`, subcommand layout, flag presence):
  clap derive on `InitArgs` + leaves. Unchanged from today.
- Dynamic value completion (`--account=<TAB>` against
  accounts in user config): `ArgValueCompleter` attached to
  the leaf in `src/options_flags/`. Already enabled via
  `clap_complete`'s `unstable-dynamic` feature. The leaf is
  the right home — completion is a clap-aware concern with
  no place in `InitOptions`.

Ops layer stays clap-free; leaf layer stays clap-aware.
Completion drives nothing in the ops layer.

### The contained wart

`From<&InitArgs> for InitOptions` walks leaf nesting once
per subcommand:

```rust
impl From<&InitArgs> for InitOptions {
    fn from(a: &InitArgs) -> Self {
        Self {
            target: a.target.clone(),
            account: a.account.account.clone(),
            scope: a.scope.scope,
            private: a.provision.private.private,
            // ...
        }
    }
}
```

Verbose but contained: one site per subcommand. Adding a new
flag becomes a two-edit change (clap struct + options struct
+ one conversion line). Accepted price of decoupling; bodies
are flat thereafter.

### What this is *not*

- **Not a god `Context`.** Two parameters
  (`&Workspace`, `&XOptions`), not one merged blob.
- **Not premature trait-based DI.** Concrete `Workspace` +
  `*Options` structs until a second front-end forces
  generalization.
- **Not a crate split.** Same crate, separate modules
  (e.g. `src/ops/init.rs` vs CLI edge). Promote to a
  workspace only when a second consumer crate appears.
- **Not removing `src/options_flags/`.** Leaves stay; only
  their consumers change shape.

### Migration sketch

The bot thinks the safest first move is converting one
subcommand end-to-end as the worked example before any
sweep. `init` is the largest surface; `sync` or `chid` would
be a lighter proof of concept.

1. Introduce `ops::Workspace` (paths + `UserConfig` only; no
   progress sink yet). Wire it into one subcommand.
2. Introduce `XOptions` for the chosen first subcommand;
   write `From<&XArgs> for XOptions`; port the body to
   `&Workspace, &XOptions`.
3. Add typed errors + returned outcome for that subcommand.
4. Sweep remaining subcommands; each is a contained step.
5. (Later) introduce `ProgressSink` when a TUI need actually
   surfaces, or a long-running op wants structured progress.

The bot thinks the right shape of `Workspace` matters more
than the right shape of `InitError` — defer error-type and
outcome-type design until step 1 has shaken out.

### Open questions

- `Workspace` carries the progress sink from day one even
  with no consumer? The bot's guess is no — leave it out and
  add when a real long-running op forces it; adding later is
  mechanical.
- `XOptions` owns values (`String`) or borrows (`&str`)? The
  bot's guess is owns — simpler, matches the programmatic-
  caller path (build then call). Defer borrow optimization
  until benchmarks call for it.
- Crate split timing — defer until a second consumer crate
  appears.
